data.title: S&P 500 Was Due For A Tougher Year In '26
data.description:
This blog post was written in late December ’25 just to think through the various variables impacting equity market returns, but one of the easier ways to analyze historical market returns is to just assume that double-digit returns will not extend forever.

After three years of actual returns for the S&P 500 of:

  • ’25: +17.88%
  • ’24: +24.87%
  • ’23: +26.37%

it would be safe to assume that just a “reversion to the mean” type year for the S&P 500 would be single digits, either mildly negative or mildly positive.

What was interesting about 2025 is the strong returns of formerly dormant asset classes like international equity (and bonds too) and emerging market equity.

These so-called non-correlated trades went nowhere for years - international equity had its best year since 2006 - in 2025.

Now, Iran has changed the 2026 investment landscape entirely, and has tossed a wrench into the “rotational” trade that made sense, at least until the air strikes.

The difficulty is trying to distinguish stocks, sectors, and asset classes, which might suffer real impairment from Iran as the US works through this conflict, from the stocks, sectors, and asset classes that are simply correcting normally, albeit driven by the headlines.

The “Liberation Day” correction that happened from late January ’25 through early April ’25 saw a 20% peak-to-trough correction.

That’s the last period where investors saw a good dose of fear in terms of investor sentiment.

Looking at historical returns for the S&P 500, here’s the last period the S&P 500 saw similar returns to ’23 – ’25:

  • ’21: +28.75%
  • ’20: +18.2%
  • ’19: +31.8%

Except for 2019, these S&P 500 annual returns were monetary policy and “zero interest rate” driven, but guess what the S&P 500 returned in 2022?

’22: -18.11%

Quick summary: Someone once referred to the S&P 500 patterns of return as “sequencing of returns” and it’s a valid point, but the larger point is that after 2 -3 years of robust equity market returns, investors tend to see single-digit numbers.

That’s not a prediction, but patterns matter.

The US equity market probably needs a good dose of extreme fear to put in a tradeable bottom after the spike in crude oil.

None of this is advice or a recommendation, but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Readers should gauge their own comfort with portfolio volatility and adjust accordingly.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: D-Wave Quantum: Proof Of Commercialization With Rare Optionality
data.description:
D-Wave Quantum's (QBTS) stock performance has been very polarizing, with a 211% return in 2025 before falling 28% YTD. However, we feel that D-Wave's gains in commercial traction are uniquely differentiated from other quantum computing peers, as its recently acquired Quantum Circuits has engendered a dual-platform positioning that few other quantum pure-plays can match.

That said, the quantum computing group as a whole is highly speculative, and D-Wave is a high-risk, high-conviction long, while trading at high valuations bereft of profits and with dilution risks.

A revenue base that starts small but is real

D-Wave reported FY2025 revenue of $24.6M, up 179% from FY2024's $8.8M. By segment, system sales achieved $16.2M, Quantum Computing as a Service contributed $5.5M, while $2.7M came from professional services. GAAP gross margins expanded 19.6 percentage points Y/Y to 82.6%.

We note that revenue in FY25 was front-loaded, as the first quarter was $15M, driven by the Jülich system sale. If we back out the remaining nine months, it would be $9.6M or $3.2M per quarter. This works out to be ~45% Y/Y if we exclude the one-off, lumpy revenue spike in 1Q25. In 4Q25 alone, revenue was $2.8M, having increased 19.2% Y/Y. While we would not penalize a nascent company like QBTS for having lumpy revenue, we advise caution in simply reading headline, annualized figures.

In addition, the company does not engage in circular financing, as stated in its opening remarks on the earnings call:

We do not give, grant, invest or lend funds to any of our customers that they utilize or intend to utilize towards the purchase of our products and/or services.

CFO John Markovich

This should help put to rest some of the concerns over quantum stocks' revenue quality. It served over 135 individual customers in FY25, with over 70 commercial customers. Needless to say, we believe that their customer base is well diversified for a relatively small company.

Inflection is in the bookings

While we are encouraged by the revenue story in FY25, we detect a more valuable signal in its bookings data. An eight-figure system sale in FY24 boosted total annual bookings during that year to $23.9M, making its FY25 bookings, which totaled $18.7M, look quite soft. However, bookings in 4Q25 were $13.4M, which was the second-highest quarter for the company. Further, QBTS closed an additional $32.8M in bookings in 1Q26. This included a $20M sale to Florida Atlantic University and a two-year, $10M enterprise QCaaS agreement with a Fortune 100 firm.

As noted by the CFO on the earnings call, prospective transactions are likely to experience a seven-fold surge along with a potential 1,500% Y/Y growth in its pipeline in FY26E.

Market consensus puts FY26E revenue at $43.6M, which reflects a 77% Y/Y growth figure. This looks very attainable to us, as more than half of that projection is likely closed under the 1Q26 sales/bookings.

Quantum Circuits acquisition to shift the calculus

In January 2026, D-Wave finished its acquisition of Quantum Circuits for ~$550M, comprised of $300M in stock and $250M in cash. This acquisition aims to solve a full range of its customers' complex computational problems with its annealing and gate-model quantum computing systems, whose build-out will be significantly accelerated by this combination.

The strategy employed here is straightforward. D-Wave has been the pioneer for annealing quantum computing for over two decades and is optimized for combinatorial problems, notably in applications for logistics, scheduling, financial modeling, and defense. On the other hand, gate-model quantum computing is the broader paradigm pursued by its peers, IonQ (IONQ), Rigetti (RGTI), IBM (IBM), and Google (GOOGL). The latter is used in drug discovery, materials science, and machine learning. This business combination increases the latitude of QBTS' addressable market.

CEO Alan Baratz described on the latest call the meaningfully differentiated technology Quantum Circuits brings. It uses a dual-rail qubit architecture, which achieves logical qubit ratios of roughly 1 logical qubit per 100-200 physical qubits, compared to approximately 1:1,000-2,000 in conventional transmon-based superconducting designs. Gate speeds are a thousand times faster without compromising on fidelity. Also, the company demonstrated on-chip cryogenic control of gate-model qubits in January this year, which the management touted as an industry first and a key enabler in scaling.

But of course, we should be mindful of execution risks. In its current laboratory stage, the company still needs to bring it into a commercially viable product alongside its established annealing business. We are hopeful that the company can build a prevailing moat around its dual-platform model.

Gaining traction among defense/government verticals

The company entered into a collaboration with Anduril Industries and Davidson Technologies in January 2026, which strengthens its position in the U.S. air and missile defense arena. D-Wave's Stride hybrid solver was notably faster and had a higher mitigation rate compared to classical-only methods. The CEO mentioned during the same earnings call that the Anduril demonstration had helped the product garner a very significant inflow of interest.

D-Wave is not simply going after research grants; it is aiming to address a sizable defense and federal optimization market with a dedicated business unit. This contrasts with other peers who target government subsidies for their research, which may never be commercially viable.

Making sense of the financials

Quite frankly, GAAP statements are not helping in analyzing the future profitability of companies like QBTS with early-stage products. We certainly appreciate that the company has already reached an initial commercialization stage with small but meaningful revenue. On profitability, we strip out non-cash/non-operating charges relating to its fair value changes in its warrant liabilities (gets worse if stock price goes up), and we get an adjusted net loss of $84.5M or -$0.26 per share in FY25. The net loss widened from FY24's $75.6M.

Working our way up the income statement, we have adjusted EBITDA at a loss of -$71.8M in FY25. On a GAAP basis, operating expenses were divided into $50.7M in R&D, $41.2M in G&A, and $28.8M in S&M, which totals $120.7M. Adjusted (non-GAAP) operating expenses were reduced to $92.9M, as $21.9M of stock-based compensation was taken out.

Consolidated operating expense is guided to grow ~15% per quarter throughout FY26. This is mainly due to the integration of Quantum Circuits, new hiring, and the Boca Raton R&D center development. Our math points to a quarterly run rate of near $50M by 4Q26E, from ~$35M starting in 1Q26E. Given market projections of $43.6M in revenue for FY26E, we believe adjusted EBITDA will worsen to a net loss of -$130M.

Balance sheet and dilution risks

D-Wave ended the year with ~$885M in cash and marketable securities. During FY25, the company raised over $800M through selling shares on the market, an ELOC facility, and warrant exercises. Total debt is minimal at $7.6M

Adjusting for the Quantum Circuits purchase, the company should have $635M cash in January. We compute a conservative cash burn rate of $25M per quarter based on the sequential operating expense increase guidance. The current cash pile can serve about 6 years of runway. On the same earnings call, CFO Markovich appears confident about bringing the company to profitability before cash runs out.

The company filed a $330M shelf, hinting further additional raises through equity are imminent, following a massive dilution in FY25, which saw shares outstanding grow 35% Y/Y even before the share raise for the Quantum Circuits deal.

Peer comparison

The whole quantum pure-play group trades absent of positive earnings, with only P/S as the only comparable metric. That said, P/S ratios are all too high to be meaningfully compared.

D-Wave emphasizes that it has a diverse set of customers, including commercial ones. But, it has a large customer covering 67% of total revenue in FY25, most likely from the Jülich system sale. IonQ doesn't disclose total customers, but the top 3 customers covered 61% of revenue in 9M25. Rigetti is essentially a government contractor, with over 90% of total revenue from the government in FY25.

Metric D-Wave IonQ Rigetti
FY2025 Revenue $24.6M $130M $7.1M
FY2025 Rev Growth +179% Y/Y +202% Y/Y -34% Y/Y
Market Cap (approx.) ~$6.9B ~$12.6B ~$5.6B
P/S (TTM) ~280x ~100x ~800x
Cash Position $884.5M $2,392M $444M
FY2026E Revenue $43.6M $225-245M (guided) $23M

Source: Companies, Himalayas Research estimates

Risks

Question of quantum supremacy. While research reported (also here) by Science confirmed D-Wave's core scientific claim with its Advantage2 system, it did not excel in all scenarios. A method using a competing classical approach produced results with less errors than the quantum annealer in certain cases involving 2D and 3D systems. However, there was significant doubt classical approaches could outperform in infinite dimension systems.

Timeline risk. There are skeptics that believe quantum error correction is near impossible to fix or may be decades away. If the quantum thesis takes longer to realize than the market judges, the entire industry will de-rate.

Dilution risk. As mentioned before, the company has recently filed a $330M shelf, and in line with its dilution history in the past, the stock will have a bigger hurdle to clear as outstanding shares keep increasing.

Verdict

In our view, D-Wave is a commercially advanced quantum pure-play with a growing, diverse set of customers riding on its unique dual-platform strategy. Revenue, despite being small, is genuine and is a strong proof of concept of commercialization. Its balance sheet is strong but comes at a dilution risk for its shareholders.

Near-term financials are not indicative of where this stock or industry trades, so P/S ratios are meaningless.

We think QBTS is a speculative BUY for investors who want optionality in quantum computing. In that case, QBTS offers an optimal bet as it is a company on solid footing, with a proven product and encouraging financials for an upstart. Positive catalysts to watch out for—1) conversion of its bookings into revenue for FY26E, 2) delivery and customer uptake of its initial gate-model system in late 2026, and 3) more government and defense contracts beyond Anduril.

data.title: CRAK: How The Conflict In Iran Is Playing In Favor Of Oil Refineries
data.description:
There is an ETF that is standing out in the energy landscape in positive terms, with a six-month return above +30%, directly linked to the energy shock following the opening of the conflict in Iran: we are talking about the VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK). Exactly… the increase in the crack spread and the presence of a favorable political and market context seem to be generating a distinctive revaluation for the prices of the companies that constitute this ETF, even compared to more generalized energy segments such as the one represented, for example, by the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE). By conducting an analysis on three levels of drivers and on two possible scenarios, I tend to remain positive in a prospective sense for this ETF. But before getting into the heart of the issue…

What is CRAK?

It is a sectoral and thematic equity ETF focused on global oil refineries that uses the MVIS Global Oil Refiners Index as its benchmark with an expense ratio of 0.62%. A cost covered by the dividend yield of 1.60% (not distinctive but still positive).

The index strategy

This index is rules-based and modified capitalization weighted and tracks the performance of the shares of companies involved in crude oil refining and in the production of products such as gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, fuel oil, naphtha, and other petrochemicals. In fact, companies must generate at least 50% of their revenues from crude oil refining to be included. They must then have a market cap above $150 million, a 3-month average daily trading volume of at least $1 million, and a minimum monthly volume of 250,000 shares over the last six months. In the ETF, the weighting method used is modified market capitalization weighting, with a maximum weight per single stock equal to 8%

It should therefore be read as a tool to invest in refining margins and in crack spreads, not as a linear proxy for the price of oil.

The holding distribution

The fund today incorporates fewer than 30 holdings, which implies a fairly concentrated distribution toward a few solutions. Not surprisingly, the top 10 is about 60% of the portfolio: a non-negligible element. Of these, 88.35% are large cap, 11.52% mid cap, and essentially no small cap exposure.

Likewise, the sector focus that emerges is clearly extreme: in practice, 100% is in the Energy sector, to which a well-distributed global geographic diversification is added. In fact, CRAK is not dominated by the United States, and over 70% of the exposure is international. So I want to say that the fund incorporates not only an industrial cycle, but also geographic, geopolitical, and currency risk.

Peers

To understand the difference, in my opinion, it is essential to make a conceptual comparison, not between specific tickers but between CRAK and traditional energy/classic oil ETFs. This is because the key difference that emerges is that CRAK does not invest generically in oil & gas, integrated majors or upstream, but in refineries, and it follows that (at least in technical terms) its main driver is not the price of crude oil itself, but the crack spread. Hence, the divergence in performance compared to the broader and more commonly used XLE, also with a generalized energy theme.

And it is in this sense interesting, indeed introductory, to note how CRAK has maintained periods of clear outperformance, such as the one that began in 2025.

But also periods of clear underperformance, such as the one that began in 2020 and continued from 2022 until precisely the end of 2025.

Catalysts

The portfolio built in this way has been described as highly exposed to crack spreads, global fuel demand, bottlenecks in refining capacity, and regional differentials between feedstock and refined products. In other words, the portfolio should, in theory, perform well when the favorable differential between crude oil and refined products widens.

In fact, in theory, the temporary decrease in refining capacity pushes the prices of refined fuels (diesel, gasoline) relative to crude oil, increasing the profitability of refiners. Since refining reacts differently compared to oil upstream, the performance of the refining segment could outperform the broader energy sector in this phase.

The other two drivers

To understand this, it is necessary to read the historical periods that we have gone through from 2016 to today (the last decade). This reading alone is enough to understand that what drives CRAK’s return is not simply the crack spread alone. This table should summarize it well.

It seems clear to me that the drivers to consider, beyond the crack spread, are the structure of the energy cycle and the pricing itself of the equity markets. So let’s try to conduct a broader context analysis:

  1. Crack spreads are widening, the profitability of refineries increases, the first point in favor of CRAK. The shock in Iran is hitting refined products, so there is a geopolitical catalyst to consider.

  2. We are in a phase of a positive energy cycle, like the one of the Trump cycle 1 of 2016-2020, in which a favorable energy context for the oil sector is present.

  3. We are in a context of sector rotation toward defensive segments, among which energy plays in favor of the expansion of the multiples of ETFs like CRAK, which in recent years have instead lagged behind the tech sector.

A scenario analysis

If, therefore, there are hypothetically two possible scenarios, which of these is more likely?

Simply put, since all three growth drivers of CRAK are positive, in my opinion, it is the structural scenario. And trying to contextualize, I expect in the short term a continuation of the current positive reaction, in parallel with the duration of the conflict, for the CRAK ETF; in the medium term, the reaction is more uncertain on the crack spread, but there still remains a favorable energy context, and an expansionary pricing of the equity markets.

Risk

Naturally, the energy shock of the conflict may be short-lived, or in any case, the increase in the crack spread may not justify an increase in the profitability of refiners. It certainly would not be the first time this happened, and 2022 is an example. After the energy shock caused by the Russian sanctions inspired by the US, Russian diesel blocked by the EU embargo was replaced by supplies from the US, the Middle East, India, China, and Korea. In other words, there was no real shock in supply, even though the crack spread had increased. This type of reorganization (re-export and reallocation) contained the impact on refining margins, leaving room for other energy companies to benefit from the increase in energy prices to the detriment of the average annual returns of CRAK.

In this sense, the shock on margins would be transitory, and the market would correct its aim on the valuations of CRAK stocks, while the general increase in energy inflation would widen the revenues of traditional energy companies like those in XLE.

This confirms the fact that statistically, crack spreads historically return to their average (typically $10-$20) and distillate inventories rebuild quickly. Therefore, the tendency to have extra profits on the part of the companies in CRAK is not a structural element, but rather a transitory one. Despite this, the cycles of widening and narrowing have allowed CRAK to maintain a linearity in returns compared to XLE, which significantly reduces the specific risk, accentuating, in my opinion, the expected risk-return ratio, pushed higher by the three positive drivers.

Conclusion

Today, however, the situation clearly appears different: the energy shock does not come from sanctions but from the conflict itself. It will therefore be the duration of the conflict that determines the extent of the widening in the profitability of the companies within the CRAK ETF. But despite this variable, there are also three other drivers not to forget: a stimulating energy context, like the one of 2016–2020 of the first Trump administration, and a process of sector rotation toward defensive segments, such as energy itself. In this sense, I therefore feel aligned with the SA Quant rating in giving a Buy rating to the CRAK ETF.

data.title: Silver Rejects Triangle Formation From Iran War - Breakout Scenarios
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Ongoing war flows have been very different from what the trading and investing world has been accustomed to.

Safe-haven assets like metals (particularly gold and silver) and bonds have not seen any consistent demand. At the same time, stock markets and risk sentiment in general have remained very solid despite stagflationary fears.

Only oil and, consequently, the petrodollar, have managed to grab consistent attention and generate persistent trends.

One question remains for metals aficionados – where is the flight to demand towards them?

Their previous run higher throughout their borderline insane performance from mid-2025 to January 2026 could explain exhausted buyers.

When people get heavily positioned in an asset class, buyers run out of bullets (to buy more), supply increases to capture the higher values, and it distorts the previous stock shortage that led to previous price increases. This heavy positioning was outlined in the recent Bank of America survey.

As a matter of fact, the ongoing war could be what is holding silver and metals prices at current levels – after the late January price tumble, their correction suddenly stalled at a similar time that WTI began to rally and the ongoing US-Iran war began to enter market discussions.

So that could open the door for a large correction in the event of a war resolution!

Still, the global economy is largely different than it was just a few years ago, with the second round of Trump Trade Wars, de-globalization and persistent rise of new conflicts.

It wouldn't be surprising to never see $20 silver prices again, but $50 is not completely out of the picture. In the meantime, traders can profit from the hesitant price action by playing ongoing consolidation around the asset classes.

We will dive into a silver multi-timeframe analysis to spot the current range in the metal and explore potential breakout levels. Let's get right into it.

Silver (XAG/USD) Multi-timeframe Technical Analysis

Daily Chart

Silver has now remained in an $80 to $96 range since the end of February, holding the top of its 2025 (broken) upward channel as support – a major technical indicator to track bull strength.

RSI momentum has died down in the higher timeframe, indicating current equilibrium around current prices. This confirms after the failed breakdown in early March and consequent failed breakout.

This provides quality indications for a range-bound price action.

Let's take a closer look

4H Chart and Technical Levels

Looking closer, bears are entering at the highs of the triangle formation, pointing to interesting tests coming ahead:

Breaking the $84.50 support trendline (from the 2025 channel) would hint at the $80 support

  • Holding the support would confirm the range towards a $90 rebound
  • Breaking it, however, could lead to retesting the $70 2026 support

Rebounding on the support trendline could lead to an imminent rebound towards the $90 to $92 resistance (less probable with current RSI conditions)

Levels to watch for Silver (XAG) trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $87 session highs (mini-resistance)
  • Key Range Resistance $90 to $92
  • Higher Timeframe Major Resistance $90 to $95
  • $96.47 March highs

Support Levels:

  • Triangle Support $84.50 (short-term bearish below)
  • 2025 Record Pivot $80 to $82
  • Key Momentum Support $76 to $77.50
  • Major 2026 Support $70 to $72
  • December FOMC Minor Support $60 to $64 (Feb Lows)
  • $50 to $54 Major Support

1H Chart

With the hesitation also spreading to the shorter timeframes, traders could await for either:

A break below the mini-support $84.50 to play a breakdown to $80

  • Ongoing selling should take the action there soon!

A break above the daily $87.50 to play a breakout to $92

Original Post

data.title: Cisco Q2: The Valuation Makes No Sense
data.description:
On Feb 11, 2026, Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) reported second-quarter FY26 earnings that, on paper, looked like a clean win. Revenue came in at $15.35 billion, beating estimates by 1.49%. Diluted EPS (non-GAAP), meanwhile, landed at $1.04 a share, topping estimates by 1.75%.

More importantly, both numbers cleared the higher end of Cisco’s own guidance, and this quarter marked a record revenue for the company.

But the stock dropped about 9% after the results went out. At the same time, Cisco is positioning itself at the center of AI infrastructure buildouts.

That, and with the other developments outlined in this analysis, my Buy rating for Cisco remains in place.

Cisco Stock Profile & Valuation

Before getting into the financials, it would make sense to see where the stock stands today and whether the price tag is justified.

At the time of this publication, Cisco trades at around $78 per share. Year-to-date, the stock is up about 1.4% or so, but zooming out to a 1-year scale shows a return of roughly 29%, and a >60% return over the past 5 years.

But of course, growth isn’t linear, at least when it comes to stocks, and we can see it in the days following earnings. Though the broader trend is still up and is moving in the right, or let me say, in a profitable direction.

In terms of valuation metrics, Cisco currently trades at a forward P/E (GAAP) of about 25.43x, which is approximately 13% below the sector median.

This means investors are paying $25.43 for every $1 of Cisco’s expected earnings, and, by traditional measures, the company is trading at a discount relative to its peers.

To me, this is noteworthy considering that this company generates over $60 billion in annual revenue and sits at the inner circle of AI infrastructure development. Yet, for some reason, the company is trading at a discount to the average tech logo. That, to me, screams undervaluation, which generally doesn’t last long.

Now, Cisco’s PEG ratio (TTM) sits at 1.26x, and this is about 41% above the sector median. That might look overly expensive at first glance, but the trailing earnings growth rate is being measured against a sector full of mature tech companies growing at low single digits. Cisco, on the other hand, delivered 31% EPS growth.

Moreover, most of the AI revenue is yet to be recognized, since Cisco’s big ramp starts in FY26. So, the least common denominator in that PEG calculation is capturing a transition period and not the real run-rate growth this business is heading to. Once AI revenue starts coming in, I see the PEG compressing pretty quickly.

With that in mind, let’s get into the business stands today.

What Was In Cisco's Second Quarter Fiscal 2026 Report?

In the most recent quarter, revenue rose 10% to $15.3 billion, with the heavy lifting coming from the product side, which rose 14% year over year to $11 billion. And of course, the star of the show was networking, growing at 21% to $8.3 billion. This is the segment that sells AI networking equipment, so a 21% increase potentially tells us that customers are spending heavily on the pipelines that carry the data supporting AI workloads.

On the profitability side, operating income rose 21% to $3.8 billion, with operating margins expanding 2.3 percentage points to 24.6%. Operating expenses, on the other hand, rose a bit by 3% to $6.2 billion. This encompasses everything from R&D spending to admin costs. As a result, net income rose 31% to $3.2 billion, or $0.80 a share (GAAP).

One thing worth flagging, though. Cisco’s effective tax rate dropped to 12.9% (from 15.9%), which partially boosted its bottom-line figures for the quarter. That lower tax rate may not necessarily be something we can count on repeating every quarter, so it’s worth keeping it in mind when projecting forward earnings.

So, if Cisco beat estimates and posted record revenue, what happened to the stock?

Why Did Cisco's Stock Drop After Earnings?

I think the selloff potentially boils down to two things: next quarter’s GAAP EPS guidance and non-GAAP gross margins guidance.

Specifically, GAAP EPS guidance for the third quarter of FY26 is between $0.73 and $0.77, which would become a sequential decline from the $0.80 Cisco just reported. Management is saying that Cisco will be less profitable in the next quarter compared to the recent one. Of course, nobody wants to hear that, especially after reporting a banner quarter. And no, the market didn’t like it.

Another one is its non-GAAP gross margin guidance, and I think investors are also concerned about it. The next quarter’s guided non-GAAP gross margin is set to come in at 65.5% and 66.5%, which is also a sequential step down from the current 67.5%. Well, guided margins aside, the reported GAAP and non-GAAP margins also experienced notable setbacks, mainly due to rising memory chip costs. As CFO Mark Patterson stated during the earnings call:

Total non-GAAP gross margin came in at 67.5%, down 120 basis points year-over-year. Non-GAAP product gross margin was 66.4%, down 130 basis points, primarily driven by negative impacts from mix and higher memory costs, partially offset by productivity improvements.

It makes sense. Memory chips are key components in networking hardware, and when prices go up, Cisco’s cost of goods moves in lockstep. Management noted that advanced purchase commitments increased by $1.8 billion in the last 90 days alone, mainly to lock in memory supply, so they’re quite upfront and transparent about it.

On that subject, the increase in cost is completely out of Cisco’s control, which is why I think the post-earnings selloff was an overreaction. You wouldn’t cut a $1.8 billion check to secure components if you expected costs to ease anytime soon, would you?

That covers the near-term pain. On the other side of the equation, there’s a significant growth catalyst that, I believe, is underappreciated and is why I think Cisco’s valuation is disjointed.

What Could Push The Stock Higher?

The reason to own Cisco right now is the sheer size of the AI infrastructure opportunity in its order book. Specifically, AI orders from hyperscale and cloud customers for the quarter increased by more than 61% to $2.1 billion compared to the same period last year.

That’s an impressive pace, and as a result, management raised annual AI order expectations to over $5 billion. Should guidance materialize as expected, it would imply a nearly 140% year-over-year increase in annual AI-related orders.

Apart from hyperscale customers, Cisco was able to diversify AI order backlogs to smaller entities. According to CEO Charles Robbins:

Beyond hyperscalers, we have a separate AI opportunity across neocloud, sovereign and enterprise customers. We took $350 million in AI orders from these customers in Q2 and have a growing pipeline in excess of $2.5 billion for our high-performance AI infrastructure portfolio. We continue to develop our strategic partnerships to capture this opportunity.

I think this diversification is a critical development that isn’t fully priced in. Now, a backlog is only worth something if there’s real evidence it’ll convert into actual revenue. During the quarter, Cisco shipped its 1-millionth Silicon One chip and introduced the 102.4 terabits-per-second G300 chip, which is, by far, the most powerful networking chip Cisco has ever made.

Cisco also launched the P200 product line, which is another high-performance networking chip. Now, it’s worth noting that neither the G300 nor the P200 is included in the $5 billion AI order target yet, so the headline number everyone is anchoring doesn’t account for demand from Cisco’s newest products. This is an underappreciated upside catalyst sitting outside the official forecasts.

On the contracts side, Cisco’s total remaining performance obligations, or RPOs, have increased 5% to $43.4 billion year-over-year. This is the total dollar value of contracts Cisco has already signed but hasn’t yet recognized as revenue.

Within the total RPO, there’s the long-term product RPO, which increased 11% year-over-year to $11.8 billion. So, Cisco has $43.4 billion in committed future revenue, with a product-specific portion that’s growing faster than the total. That’s a good sign of product demand.

Now, management expects to recognize over $3 billion in AI revenue from hyperscalers alone in FY’26. If Cisco delivers on that guidance and order momentum holds, the AI infrastructure segment alone could make up 5 to 6% of the company’s total revenue. It’s a small slice of the pie, indeed, but considering Cisco’s AI revenue was basically nonexistent about 2 years ago, that’s a notable change in revenue mix.

And considering that the G300 and P200s are still in the early innings, I believe AI orders could exceed the guided $5 billion in FY’27. Personally, I think the payoff also shows up on the earnings line. For example, Cisco’s OpEx increased only 3% this quarter, so the cost is barely moving. When the backlog starts converting to recognized revenue at current product margins (63 to 64%), a much larger portion of the revenue will flow straight to the bottom lines.

What's The Risk Around Cisco?

Now, with every investment opportunity, it’s critical to take a balanced view of what could go wrong. The core issue Cisco faces is rising memory costs. According to reports, High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) prices have surged ~400% year-over-year and could rise even higher. Well, management has acknowledged that these increases hit with virtually zero lead time. In fact, this is the primary driver behind the 120 basis point decline in non-GAAP gross margin in the recent quarter and the expected sequential decline in the next quarter.

Cisco is actively working to offset this. In fact, it was managed using three levers: price increases for customers, revised contract terms with partners, and taking advantage of Cisco’s purchasing power in supplier negotiations, which resulted in the 73% year-over-year increase in inventory. To me, these are sensible moves, and I expect them to gradually take hold.

The reality, though, is that Patterson also acknowledged these measures take time to flow through. He said during the earnings call:

And then in terms of the timing, I think the measures that we talked about in terms of price increases and then some of the Ts and Cs that we're going to be modifying with our partners and customers, those just take a little bit of time to run through, so you'll start to see that over time.

The thing is, Cisco’s fastest-growing segment is the AI infrastructure, which also happens to be the most memory-intensive. So, as AI orders ramp up, memory exposure ramps up with them. No, I don’t think this dynamic will last forever, as supply commitments will eventually catch up. But, in the near term, it does create a situation where revenue growth can coexist with margin pressure.

What's My Verdict For Cisco?

The debate right now is about the next 2 quarters. Will margins finally stabilize? Will the cost of memory ease? Both are fair arguments, but they are short-term. I think the bigger picture is that Cisco is positioning itself to be one of those that sell the entire foundation layer of how AI gets connected globally.

Apart from what the numbers already show, what gives me confidence is the simple observation about how AI spending is maturing. Right now, the market rewards those who are selling picks and shovels- in this case, it’s the GPUs and chips, but the bottleneck is gradually moving downstream.

The networking layer potentially becomes the next bottleneck, and that’s where Cisco comes into play. On that note, I think the market is still pricing the company as a secondary beneficiary of AI spending, even when industry trends suggest it’s becoming a primary one.

With that and everything else outlined above, my Buy rating for Cisco stays in place.

data.title: ​Silver Price Retreats Toward $85 After CPI Keeps Rates And Oil In Focus
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) traded firmer on Thursday, March 12, holding near $87 after a bruising midweek reversal, as buyers returned to the market but stopped short of forcing a full breakout. The metal found some support after Wednesday’s slide, yet the broader setup remained tense with oil back near triple digits, the dollar close to its strongest levels of the year, and Treasury yields still elevated.

Silver is trying to rebuild after failing to hold the push toward $89 earlier this week. Thursday’s trade put the market back on firmer footing, but the rebound still looks more like stabilization than a decisive turn, with the recent high area still acting as the first meaningful resistance zone.

Support now comes into view around $85, with a lower floor near $84.50 after that level drew buyers during the latest washout. If silver stays above that band, the market may keep settling into a tighter short-term range. A move back through $88 would improve the near-term picture, though buyers would still need to clear the recent upper boundary to regain stronger control.

The chart remains lively because silver is still trading far above where it started the year. In that kind of environment, failed rallies matter more than usual, since they can quickly pull momentum traders back into profit-taking.

When the bounce reaches the first test

Silver is trying to rebuild after failing to hold the push toward $89 earlier this week. Thursday’s trade put the market back on firmer footing, but the rebound still looks more like stabilization than a decisive turn, with the recent high area still acting as the first meaningful resistance zone.

Support now comes into view around $85, with a lower floor near $84.50 after that level drew buyers during the latest washout. If silver stays above that band, the market may keep settling into a tighter short-term range. A move back through $88 would improve the near-term picture, though buyers would still need to clear the recent upper boundary to regain stronger control. The chart remains lively because silver is still trading far above where it started the year. In that kind of environment, failed rallies matter more than usual, since they can quickly pull momentum traders back into profit-taking.

Macro pressure has not left the room

The inflation picture offered little fresh relief. February consumer prices rose 2.4% from a year earlier, while core inflation held at 2.5%, leaving a reading that was calm enough to avoid a new shock but not soft enough to change the broader rate story. Energy prices kept the market on edge. Oil surged again on Thursday, with Brent (CO1:COM) rising toward $100 and briefly moving higher, as renewed disruption concerns in the Gulf added to fears that energy could keep feeding inflation expectations even after this week’s consumer price report.

At the same time, the dollar stayed close to its 2026 highs and the U.S. 10-year yield remained above 4.20%. That combination does not rule out gains in silver, but it does make follow-through harder unless the market gets either calmer energy pricing or some relief in rates.

What could shape the next leg

If silver keeps holding above $85 and works back into the upper $87-88 area, the market could start looking more balanced after the latest shakeout. That would likely need a steadier oil market or some easing in yields so buyers are not fighting several macro headwinds at once. If crude stays hot and the dollar remains firm, silver may struggle to do much more than bounce inside a volatile range. A move back under $84.50 would put the recent correction back at the center of the chart and raise the risk that the market spends more time cooling before trying higher levels again.

Silver is still trading far above where it began the year, even after this week’s sharp pullback, which is why short-term volatility is likely to remain elevated. The current move matters because it will help determine whether the latest drop was a fast reset inside a larger uptrend or the start of a broader cooling phase.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Netskope: IPO Lockup Expiration Gift
data.description:
Netskope, Inc. (NTSK) is a leading AI cybersecurity and networking company, yet the stock has slumped after earnings. The company had a hot IPO, but the stock trades at the lowest levels after going public despite the strong demand for their products. My investment thesis is ultra Bullish as the lock-up expiration dip creates a strong buying opportunity.

Lock-Up Expiration

After a company goes public, a stock goes through a volatile period with the initial quarterly results and guidance not always gelling with analyst expectations. In addition, the lock-up expiration after six months tends to lead to a sell-off, with the market pre-empting potential insider sales, with Netscape having ~390 million shares hitting the market on March 13.

Netskope just reported the second quarterly results since going public, and the market potentially didn't fully grasp the sequential expectations. The AI networking company reported the January quarterly results beat analyst estimates by $7 million following the original quarter with an $8 million beat. The stock went public back on September 18, raising $908 million with the offering priced at $19.

The stock surged to open at $23 and hit a high of nearly $28 within days. Ironically, Netskope is now trading below $10 following another good quarter where results beat analyst expectations.

Netskope is at the center of the AI revolution with products managing networking security and analytics covering AI products, especially agentic traffic. The company helps speed up AI traffic while safeguarding a business from cyber threats.

The company reported FQ4 revenue grew by 32% to reach $196 million. Netskope even generated a record net new ARR of $57 million during the January quarter, with total ARR up 31% YoY to reach $811 million.

The only potential issue is the market not understanding guidance. Netskope guided to both FQ1 revenue of $198 million and FY27 revenue of $873 million, both above consensus estimates.

The market has a way to parse data differently once presented in a quarterly report. Netskope has a few potential negatives the market is sweating as follows:

  • FQ1'27 revenue guidance is relatively flat sequentially at $198 million versus $196 million in the prior quarter.
  • FY27 operating margin forecast is highly negative at (10.0%).
  • FY27 revenue guidance at $873 million is a substantial deceleration to 23% growth.
  • Shift to annual billings impacts free cash flows in FY27.
  • The company mentioned macroeconomic risks with FY27 guidance.

The big issue here is that the market really has no grasp on whether the management team provides conservative guidance or not. If the company keeps a tradition of $7 to $8 million quarterly revenue beats, the stock market wouldn't have a big problem with FQ1 revenues of $205+ million.

50% IPO Discount

The stock value has slipped to below $4 billion, with Netskope dipping to nearly $9. The IPO lock-up expiration takes place in the next week, which should free up the stock for less downside pressure, especially considering insiders are unlikely to unload shares at $10 when the IPO took place at $19.

Netskope has net cash of ~$480 million, making the EV closer to $3 billion. The stock only trades at 3x EV/S targets for FY28 with sales forecasts of $1.06 billion, a nearly unheard-of valuation in an AI security and networking company with 20%+ growth.

The stock trades at a big discount to other cybersecurity plays in the networking security market. As mentioned above, Netskope trades at close to 3x EV/S targets, while Zscaler (ZS) trades at 6x forward EV/S targets, and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) is still up at 10x targets, even after the market sell-off in cybersecurity stocks.

The biggest risk is Netskope not hitting financial targets. The company still has large operating losses, and the market will punish the stock with decelerating growth while still unprofitable.

Netskope is cash flow positive, with F26 cash flows from operations at $38 million, but the guidance is to start the year with negative cash flows. The company is forecast for a free cash flow margin of 2% to 4% for FY27, or the equivalent of ~$26 million, more than double the $12 million last year despite the impact from the shift to annual billings.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Netskope sits in an appealing segment with network security becoming a huge focus of AI traffic exploding in an agent world. The market is getting a gift of a hot IPO to trade at a 50% discount while ARR is still growing at a 30%+ rate.

data.title: EWS: Singapore Is The Artificial Intelligence Weathervane We All Need To Monitor
data.description:
The wind always blows, but with artificial intelligence, the current is ever shifting and fracturing. Will an AI bubble pop? Is AI nothing but smoke and mirrors? Will AI usher in a new epoch? Could AI solve the world’s myriad problems? Maybe, possibly, nobody knows. If anyone does know, they’re likely in an inner circle that retail investors aren’t privy to. That said, investors can watch for secondary signals, and I believe Singapore offers one of the best opportunities to do so. Singapore is becoming a leader in AI adoption, and the city-state’s highly competent government is putting its full weight behind AI initiatives.

I’ve covered the iShares MSCI Singapore ETF (EWS) before and rated it a Buy back in July 2025. EWS enjoyed strong gains for a while before cooling off. It was on the rise once again before the war with Iran kicked off. The ETF cooled from about $29.5 to $27 but has started to tick upward again. Amid the intensifying Iran conflict, as a trading hub and city-state, Singapore is in a particularly vulnerable position in many ways. As global tradewinds go, so too will Singapore. I believe the price decline right now is temporary and largely due to the war with Iran. However, I also suspect that conditions will get worse before they get better, but at the same time, I don’t expect the war to continue for an overly prolonged period of time.

Anyway, I rate Singapore a Buy and believe that AI will deliver substantial productivity gains, which will bolster Singaporean companies. I also believe that if AI does supercharge the economy and productivity in Singapore, other countries will be able to find similar success, fueling increasing adoption, which in turn will propel the AI industry as a whole. If Singapore struggles to substantially boost productivity, it could suggest that AI is smoke and mirrors, or at the very least, not ready to change the world. Singapore’s small size, high adoption rate, and effective governance make it a perfect test bed.

Before jumping into AI, however, I’d like to quickly cover some important historical details.

Singapore Ranks Among The Best-Managed Countries The World Over

Singapore has the unique distinction of being one of the few countries ever to be forcefully pushed out of its motherland. The colonial history is rather complex, but to simplify things, Singapore was used by the British as a trading and transport hub and was administered separately from the nearby Malay Peninsula. The British colonized Singapore in 1819 and took a generally light-handed approach with the native bumiputera (sons of the soil) across much of what is now Malaysia. In Singapore, rather than relying heavily on locals, the British encouraged Chinese traders to move to the island. (The British would make deeper inroads into peninsular Malaysia primarily in the latter half of the 19th century.)

Anyway, Singapore became a Chinese-dominated city, but it was attached to a Bumiputera (primarily Malay) motherland. As the British wound down their colonial empire after World War II, Singapore and the rest of Malaysia formed a federation. However, ethnic tensions between Chinese and Malays, and especially between the Malay-dominated federal government and the Chinese-dominated Singaporean government, led to Singapore being pushed out.

This is important because as soon as Singapore was pushed out, it found itself in an extremely difficult position. It had no motherland, its market was tiny, and its natural resources were scarce. This sparked an existential drive that would see Singapore abandon traditional development models, such as import substitution. In many ways, Singapore laid down the framework for globalization that would later reshape the world. The government invested heavily in key areas, like manufacturing, real estate development, and air travel, with an emphasis on exports, but was also careful to ensure a competitive market economy, thus helping prevent wasteful government projects.

Singapore still views itself as vulnerable, and the government still plays a very active role in society while emphasizing efficiency and working to avoid bloat. The government emphasizes meritocracy and offers salaries competitive with the private sector as well. In the 2010 American diplomat cables, you can find plenty of praise for the Singaporean government and its management of society and the economy. I believe this is important, because if I had to bet on a government making a successful society-wide push to encourage AI adoption, Singapore would be at the top of my list.

Singapore Blazing The Artificial Intelligence Trail

Singapore was one of the trailblazers in regard to modern development and globalization. Now, it’s aiming to pave a path with Artificial Intelligence. The city-state constantly needs to maintain a competitive edge and is surrounded by neighbors who can offer cheaper labor and more natural resources while generally being well-managed to some extent or another. The country’s population is aging and shrinking as well. These challenges encourage the Singaporean government to remain nimble and forward-looking.

The Singaporean government is working to create at least 100,000 AI bilingual workers over the next few years, meaning people with both AI skills and skills in other fields. So, you have AI bilingual accountants and legal professionals. While teachers in many countries are worried about students using AI to cheat, Singapore is setting up programs to teach kids and college students how to effectively use AI. The government is also providing free subscriptions for citizens taking select courses to test out AI.

As for businesses, the Enterprise Innovation Scheme will provide up to 400% tax deductions for AI-related expenses for businesses with a cap of up to 50,000 SGD. A forthcoming “Champions of AI Initiative” will see the government working closely alongside Singaporean companies to foster AI-related leadership skills and upskill workers. When I see governments launch these sorts of programs, I temper my expectations because they frequently fall short, but the Singaporean government has a proven track record, and if anyone can pull it off, it’s probably them.

As a result of various efforts, Singapore has, by some measures, become the world’s leader in terms of AI adoption. Cybernews ranks Singapore number 1 globally and notes that adoption has grown from 24% of citizens using AI in 2023 to 52% by 2024 and 66% by 2025. By adoption, I mean getting employees and citizens to use AI, getting management buy-in, and producing concrete results, rather than conducting research in bleeding-edge models like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO).

Singaporean businesses are reporting a 16% return on investment (ROI) with AI projects and are aiming to hit 29% within the next few years. 38% are expecting to increase AI investments, and 67% are satisfied with the outcomes of their AI projects so far. AI adoption is still rather nascent, and AI technology itself is probably rudimentary compared to what we’ll have access to in 10 years. That early returns are so promising, however, bodes well not just for Singapore but for the AI industry as well. I believe Singaporean companies will deliver on long-term productivity gains, which in turn makes EWS an especially attractive investment.

EWS: Fundamentals And Valuation Considerations

The first thing that jumps out to me with EWS is the low portfolio P/E ratio, which, according to Morningstar, weighs in at ~15.3. This P/S ratio is also quite low at ~2.3. These valuation metrics are often quite high in the United States, leading some to speculate if the stock market is getting a bit bubbly. The holdings breakdown, as shown below, suggests that this ETF is rather highly concentrated in financials. I’m a bit torn on this. I generally like to see more diversity. However, with the AI thesis, if Singapore can secure productivity gains, it should stimulate general business activity, which in turn should bolster the financial sector.

The adjusted expense ratio is quite low at .5%. I’ve reviewed a fair number of overseas ETFs, and this is one of the lower adjusted expense ratios I’ve seen. Ratios of roughly .6% or higher are pretty common. The dividend yield, as shown below, is quite attractive, topping 4%, with the 3-year CAGR quite high at 32.77%. If the economy hits a rough patch, I wouldn’t be surprised if dividends shrank rather quickly as companies circle the wagons and try to preserve cash.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant tools have been cooling on EWS. For many months, the ETF was in the green with a Buy recommendation (shown below). Recently, it slipped into Hold territory and then into Sell territory. Yet the individual grades aren’t bad but rather mediocre. Overall, I disagree with the quant tools grades, especially in light of the AI thesis. It can be hard to capture forward-looking potential with quant tools, and in a sense, I think that’s what’s happening here.

Why I Could Be Wrong

For one, Artificial Intelligence could run into some sort of ceiling. While progress has been rapid so far, we can’t really be sure of the upside. As for EWS being a Buy, Singapore is in a rather vulnerable position, and the war with Iran, as well as global trade tensions, could have an outsized impact on the city-state. As for general views of artificial intelligence, it’s possible that Singapore will turn out to be an exception rather than a rule regarding AI adoption. Perhaps Singapore will succeed while others will fail.

Key Takeaway: Keep An Eye On Singapore And A Buy On EWS

EWS offers a good mix of fundamentals and diversification from American equities. In a sense, Singapore as a whole can be viewed as an AI play, given how quickly the country is adapting and leveraging AI. Even if you’re not interested in investing in Singapore, I still recommend keeping an eye on how AI adoption goes, as it could portend the future for many other countries.

data.title: The Recent Pullback In Hingham Institution for Savings Seems Justified
data.description:
This article serves as a follow-up to my past analysis of Hingham Institution for Savings (HIFS). When I first wrote about the company in November of last year, I rated its stock as a Hold based on its elevated valuation and risk profile. Since that article was published, HIFS has risen about 7.5% while the overall market has only achieved a gain of about 2.5%. Hingham had been able to post even greater gains through early February of this year, but the stock has tumbled almost 17% since reaching a 52-week high about five weeks ago.

Much of the decline in the stock can be explained by macro factors in the market. Nonetheless, I do think that the recent pullback in HIFS is justified based on the most recent quarterly results posted by the company. Hingham’s low net interest margin and below-average operational metrics simply make it a less attractive investment option compared to other small regional banks that I have covered on this site in the last six months. I am maintaining my Hold rating for HIFS.

Company Profile

Hingham Institution for Savings is one of the oldest banks in the US, with a history that dates back almost 200 years. The bank has a rather unique geographic footprint with six branches in Massachusetts, one in San Francisco, and one in the nation’s capital of Washington, DC. Hingham has several unique services for its customers that include no fees for active accounts and unlimited deposit insurance thanks to the Massachusetts Depositors Insurance Fund.

The company has a current market cap of $610.5 million. According to the 2025 10-K filed by HIFS on March 4, Hingham’s total assets were valued at $4.54 billion at the end of last year, a modest increase of 1.9% from the end of 2024. Included in those assets were gross loans outstanding worth $3.93 billion. A full snapshot of the bank’s balance sheet data from the company’s 2025 Annual Report is shown below.

As I mentioned in my first analysis for HIFS, I have never seen another bank so committed to real estate, with 84.1% of its loan mix tied to commercial real estate and an additional 11.1% connected to home mortgages. An additional 4.0% is committed to construction loans. Non-real estate consumer and commercial loans make up less than 1% of the bank’s portfolio.

Total deposits for the bank totaled $2.59 billion at the end of Q4. The company relies heavily on wholesale funding to grow its loans. Hingham reported $1.46 in advances from the Federal Home Loan Bank at the end of last year. The average bank of a similar size to HIFS has a ratio of net loans to total deposits of 84.46%, according to the FDIC. For Hingham, that ratio is 153.0%.

The company pays a regular quarterly dividend of $0.63, an amount that has not changed since the last quarter of 2022. However, Hingham also has a history of supplementing its regular payouts with special dividends, an event that took place in January of this year when the company paid its stockholders an additional $0.70. This was the first special distribution for the company since the final quarter of 2022. When the company did not make an additional payout in 2023, it marked the first time that Hingham had not done so since 2010. Without the inclusion of the special dividend, HIFS has a forward yield of only 0.90%.

Analysis of Hingham Institution for Savings

When looking at the bottom line for HIFS in FY 2025, the company enjoyed a banner year. Basic earnings per share of $25.01 were almost double the $12.95 earned by the company in FY 2024. However, not all of that growth was operational. For example, the company reported a gain on the sale of equity securities in Q3 2025 that added $4.03 to the bottom line for HIFS in that quarter.

The company’s ability to profit from the sale of securities is not a one-time occurrence. Hingham reported a net gain on equity securities of $20.4 million in FY 2024 and followed that performance with $28.8 million in gains in FY 2025. Considering the total net income for the bank before taxes was $72.8 million, these security sales have been a meaningful contributor to Hingham's bottom line.

These sales are also clouding the true operational performance of the bank itself. Hingham reported a return on assets of 1.84% for Q4 2025 and 1.22% for the full year. The quarterly number is well above the national average for banks of its size. However, when security sales are removed from the equation, ROA drops to 0.89% for Q4 and 0.72% for FY 2025. The national average for this metric in Q4 2025 was 1.31%, showing that the actual banking operations for HIFS are not producing the same returns as typical peer banks. A similar impact on return on equity can be seen in the chart below. As a point of reference, the national average for ROE in Q4 was 11.88%.

The outstanding efficiency ratio posted by the bank in Q4 was not impacted by the securities sale. The Q4 ratio of 35.06% is well below the national mean and an improvement of over 17% from the same quarter in 2024. This is an impressive achievement by the company. However, the strong performance in holding down expenses was offset by a net interest margin that is over 50% below the national average for a bank of Hingham’s asset size.

The net interest margin of 1.89% that was reported at the end of the year was being impacted by both low loan yields and high costs for its deposits. HIFS has a loan portfolio that was yielding just 4.82% at the end of last year. The typical bank of Hingham’s size had a loan yield of over 6.5%. The bank reported a cost of interest-bearing deposits of 3.15% at the end of 2025, 46 basis points higher than the national average.

When all the pieces are added together, the banking operations for Hingham Institution for Savings are not as strong as they appear from just a quick glance at the recent profit numbers. The PE ratio for the bank of 11.3 is slightly higher than I prefer in a small-cap bank. With a tangible book value of $219.82, the stock is trading at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.24, which is not exactly in bargain territory for a bank with the substandard performance metrics mentioned earlier.

Additional Risks to Consider

When compared to the end of 2024, the asset quality for Hingham Institution for Savings has taken a noticeable downward fall. The ratio of non-performing loans to total loans stood at 0.80% at the end of 2025, a deterioration of 76 basis points over the last 12 months. Non-performing assets reached 0.69%, 66 basis points worse than at the end of FY 2024. Much of this increase is tied to one $30.6 million CRE loan that was placed on non-accrual status last year.

The bank’s allowance for credit losses has only increased by four basis points since the end of 2024, giving reason to believe that the bank may have to account for additional loan issues in the future should other loans on its books deteriorate. However, the bank has a tradition of strong lending practices, and it should be noted that HIFS has been able to avoid any charge-offs in FY 2025 or FY 2024.

The company also had to respond to a report from a short seller last month that stated the bank is “over-leveraged” and “under-reserved.” This report identified over $125 million in loans in the Washington, DC, area that may not be properly assessed as distressed. The bank has disputed these claims and affirmed the accuracy of its financial reports.

Even without the concerns that were mentioned in this report, there is no denying that Hingham has an almost 100% exposure to real estate in its loan portfolio. This makes the bank especially sensitive to issues that could make the repayment of those loans, both commercial and consumer, more challenging in the future. These issues could include rising unemployment, increasing consumer debt, a slowdown in the growth of the US economy, and stubborn inflation that unexpectedly moves higher later this year.

The bank’s quirky service area of Massachusetts, DC, and San Francisco also makes it susceptible to issues that could impact those markets specifically. According to the company’s 2025 10-K, 63.5% of the loans at the bank are secured by properties in Massachusetts. An additional 33.1% are linked to properties in Washington, DC. Recent estimates show that Massachusetts is trailing the national average for GDP growth, a trend that has been in place for the last couple of years. The causes for this include higher taxes, a rising cost of living, and resident migration to other states. Washington, DC, has also faced economic headwinds over the last year related to government cutbacks.

There is still a great deal of skepticism for the company as a whole. When I wrote my first analysis for HIFS, the stock had a short interest of 11.68%. On March 12, that number had risen to 15.71%. Whether it be the bank's low core metrics, asset quality issues, or lack of growth opportunities in its service areas, there are large bets in the market that this stock is likely to underperform in the near future.

The Bottom Line

Hingham Institution for Savings has shown some modest improvement in its overall picture since my last analysis was published. However, the valuation of its stock seems to imply a higher level of banking performance than what I have found. That fact, added in with the loan risks, is enough for me to avoid an upgrade at this time. Even with the special dividend included, its stock’s yield of 1.16% is not enough for me to wait around for these issues to improve. The short interest also raises a red flag that there may be more troubles lying ahead for the bank. I am maintaining my Hold rating on HIFS with better options available in the small-cap regional bank space.

data.title: Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Portfolio Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Touchstone Large Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Portfolio Review

The Touchstone Large Cap Fund (Class A Shares, Load Waived) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 1000® Index, for the quarter ended December 31, 2025.

The underperformance of the Quality factor, which the Fund tilts toward, was a headwind to relative performance this quarter. The best performing sectors for the Russell 1000 index were Health Care and Communications Services, while Real Estate and Utilities were the weakest sectors.

During the quarter, sector allocation and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance. An underweight to both Information Technology and Real Estate aided performance, while an underweight Health Care and overweight Financials detracted from performance.

Turning to stock selection, the best performing stocks based on relative performance vs. the benchmark during the quarter included Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (Communications Services sector), FedEx Corp. (FDX) (Industrials sector), and Chubb Ltd. (CB) (Financials sector).

Alphabet was a top performer this quarter following strong core business execution and accelerating adoption of its AI offerings. Growth was broad-based across search, YouTube, and cloud, with AI enhancing search utility and monetization while cloud margins reached new highs. Management continues to balance disciplined cost controls with increased AI investment, reinforcing confidence in long-term returns. We remain attracted to its massive ecosystem scale, sound capital allocation, and clean balance sheet.

FedEx was a top performer after reporting better results led by improved revenue quality, disciplined pricing, and continued cost savings. Despite the freight recession, domestic ground volume has been fairly resilient recently, and margins expanded in the core Express business due to the structural cost reductions. We remain attracted to the strong cash generation, balanced capital allocation, and a solid balance sheet.

Chubb was a strong performer in the fourth quarter, reaching an all-time high, following exceptional earnings that featured record core operating income, industry-leading combined ratios, and robust investment income growth. The company’s diversified portfolio, disciplined underwriting, and aggressive capital returns reinforced confidence in its ability to generate attractive returns, even as the Property and Casualty industry transitions to a more competitive and volatile environment.

The more challenged positions based on relative performance during the quarter included Fiserv Inc. (FISV) (Financials sector), NewMarket Corp. (NEU) (Materials sector), and BlackRock Inc. (BLK) (Financial Services sector).

Fiserv weakness came after ongoing concerns about a slowdown in its small business point-of-sale platform on top of weak third quarter results. The company took a steep guidance cut and announced management and Board changes. The large stock reaction reflects a loss of credibility, increased doubts around the long-term growth outlook, and the realization that a turnaround will require meaningful investment over the medium-term. Given the extent of the reset and diminished confidence in the thesis, we sold our position.

NewMarket was a weaker performer as a softer global environment pressured Petroleum Additives volumes and margins and the Specialty Materials business had lumpy demand, which weighed on results. Lower oil prices reduced operating leverage and drove an unusual restructuring charge as industry capacity increased.

Despite near-term pressure, we continue to view NewMarket as a strong business with disciplined capital allocation, balance sheet flexibility, and strong cash flow generation. Attracted to its best-in- class operations, conservative underwriting, and shareholder- friendly capital allocation philosophy.

BlackRock was a bottom performer during the quarter as investor sentiment was pressured by concerns around private market liquidity, return durability, and higher expenses tied to recent acquisitions. Despite this, fundamentals remain solid, with record AUM, healthy flows, and organic base fee growth well above long-term targets, driven by active ETFs, digital assets, and alternatives. We continue to view BlackRock as a long-term share gainer, and we appreciate the strong balance sheet and steady capital return.

In late December, we trimmed the large position in Alphabet Inc. based on strength and to comply with our stated 10% position limits. We used the funds to increase our position in Republic Services Inc. (RSG) (Industrials sector).

We added to Republic Services as it is an attractive opportunity to increase our exposure in a defensive and resilient business model. The waste management industry has lagged the broader market this year due to cyclical volume weakness in construction, demolition, and the industrial sector. Despite these short term headwinds, Republic Services continues to exhibit operational strength through pricing power and cost controls, which have led to stronger margins. We remain attracted to the high-quality, essential business with strong and stable cash flow generation.

Recently, insiders have bought shares in the open market, which we view as an additional data point to the attractive valuation.

In October, we sold the remaining Fiserv position after it hit our soft stop loss. The doubts over the new long-term algorithm and the durability of its core business are now in question. Prior management’s reliance on low-quality, short-term growth led us to lose confidence in the business. While there is still value in the core business, it is now a turnaround story that will require heavy investment to reset the strategy and restore genuine earnings growth.

In October, we used the excess cash to fund two purchases of existing holdings, Chubb Ltd. and Norfolk Southern Corp. (NSC) (Industrials sector).

We added to our Chubb position after the most recent quarterly report. Results continue to be better than expected and management increased its return on equity target. Loss trends in the industry have stabilized and premiums written should accelerate. Management also accelerated the buyback program and signaled that the stock is trading under its intrinsic value. We remain confident in the attractive nature Chubb business.

We added to our Norfolk Southern position after the shares pulled back despite the pending Union Pacific deal. It is currently trading at a discount to the value of the Union Pacific deal and the rail industry is out of favor. While most recent earnings were softer than expected Norfolk Southern has shown signs of improving its execution and volumes should return incrementally. Lastly, the chairman stepped up and purchased shares in the open market, which we view as a sign of conviction in the business.

A Word About Risk

The Fund invests in equities which are subject to market volatility and loss. The Fund invests in stocks of large-cap companies which may be unable to respond quickly to new competitive challenges. The Fund may invest a greater percentage of its assets in the securities of a limited number of issuers and may be subject to greater risks. The Fund may focus its investments in specific sectors and therefore is subject to the risk that adverse circumstances will have greater impact on the fund than on the fund that does not do so. Events in the U.S. and global financial markets, including actions taken to stimulate or stabilize economic growth may at times result in unusually high market volatility, which could negatively impact Fund performance and cause it to experience illiquidity, shareholder redemptions, or other potentially adverse effects. Banks and financial services companies could suffer losses if interest rates rise or economic conditions deteriorate. Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to change. The Advisor engages a sub-advisor to make investment decisions for the Fund's portfolio; it may be unable to identify and retain a sub-advisor who achieves superior investment returns relative to other similar sub-advisors.
